feature.name: Overscroll Gestures
feature.summary: Adds HTML primitives (overscrollcontainer attribute + command=toggle-overscroll via command invokers) to anchor elements to overscroll areas of containers and reveal them with swipe gestures, with a guaranteed accessible button fallback. Also incubated as 'Declarative Overscroll Actions'.

intake.finding: ChromeStatus entry created 2025-09-08 by vmpstr with a clear, specific problem statement: gesture-driven UIs (drawer/side menus, swipe-to-reveal, pull-to-refresh) are awkward to express without script today. Mode classified as a new feature under Blink>Animation, owners vmpstr@chromium.org and flackr@chromium.org, devrel routing to devrel-chromestatus-all, behind enable-experimental-web-platform-features. Explainer, initial public proposal (CSSWG), bug, devtrial instructions, and a sample were all linked at intake. The acceptance of a requested ship year is correctly NOT treated as the lifecycle stage: the feature is at Proposed/dev-trial, not shipped.
intake.limitations: Intake metadata is strong; no evidence of an explicit intake diagnosis artifact, but the ChromeStatus record carries the equivalent problem/owner/venue information.

incubation.finding: The developer/end-user problem is real, recurring, and narrowly scoped, and the team chose the right venues: an initial CSSWG proposal (2025-09-08, placed on the CSS WG TPAC 2025 agenda) followed by Open UI incubation (issue 2026-02-13) and a WHATWG HTML proposal (2026-03-17). Alternatives were genuinely considered and rejected with reasoning: a CSS-only solution was rejected because it lacks semantic enforcement and risks inaccessible 'invisible' gestures, and scroll-driven-animations/snap workarounds are noted as hard. The design deliberately evolved from pure-CSS to HTML attributes plus a new toggle-overscroll invoker command, reusing the Invoker Commands pattern. However, developer/end-user demand is asserted rather than evidenced: ChromeStatus records Web Dev 'No signals', and no survey, framework-author, or partner evidence was found. The 'why not' / counterevidence search surfaced only awareness amplification of the I/O announcement, not independent demand.
incubation.limitations: No attributable developer research, partner interest, or framework/library-author evidence found to validate demand distinct from problem validity.

prototype.finding: An Intent to Prototype was sent to blink-dev (thread confirmed; full body not retrievable). A detailed explainer with API examples, use cases (drawer, swipe-to-reveal, swipe-to-dismiss, pull-to-refresh), an implementation model, accessibility considerations, progressive-enhancement/polyfill-via-popover fallback, and open questions was published. A polyfill demo that 'does not require any browser features' is referenced, and a Chromium implementation exists behind the flag (finch name OverscrollGestures). Authors (vmpstr, flackr, mfreed7) are the continuity team behind Invoker Commands/popover, which de-risks the API-pattern reuse. Gaps: the canonical sample demo and the Chromium implementation bug could not be retrieved, so there is no independent confirmation of implementation completeness, test coverage, or friction-log state at the prototype->dev-trial handoff.
prototype.limitations: Intent-to-Prototype body, flackr sample demo, and Chromium bug (issues.chromium.org/443719046) were not retrievable via the reader; no friction-log or conformance artifact was found.

developerTrials.finding: This is the feature's current/active stage: a developer trial behind the enable-experimental-web-platform-features flag at Chrome 149. Trial infrastructure exists: devtrial instructions are linked (vmpstr/htmldemos gestures/devtrial.md), a sample is linked (flackr web-demos), and the feature is flagged and finch-named. The trial is very recent (Chrome 149 stable ~June 2026; first public DevRel mention 2026-07-01), so the phase's core decisions are unanswered: no cohort/recruitment report, no task-completion or friction findings, no attributable partner-evidence ladder, and no published design changes from trial feedback have been found. Per the phase module, this is the moment to avoid vanity counts and self-selected-enthusiast bias; instead there is simply no published denominator yet.
developerTrials.limitations: Dev-trial instructions document could not be retrieved (reader errors); no cohort findings, feedback themes, or partner evidence published or found. Too early to assess trial outcomes.

wideReview.finding: Wide review has not substantively begun and is formally pending: ChromeStatus records tag review 'Pending', security review 'Pending', privacy review 'Pending'; Firefox 'No signal'; Safari 'No signal'; Web Dev 'No signals'; standards maturity 'Unknown status'. Standards work is genuinely active but early and unresolved across venues: CSSWG issue 13999 (2026-06-01) is labeled 'Needs Edits' and 'Needs Testcase (WPT)', and the WHATWG HTML proposal (2026-03-17) is labeled 'addition/proposal', 'needs implementer interest', and 'stage: 1 Incubation'. ChromeStatus claims WPT:true but the CSSWG issue still asks for a WPT testcase, a discrepancy. Crucially, 'No signal' from other engines must NOT be read as neutral or positive: the WHATWG 'needs implementer interest' label makes the Chrome-only-period and Baseline risk explicit and unresolved.
wideReview.limitations: No TAG, Mozilla, or WebKit position statements found; all formal Chrome reviews pending; spec text not yet stable (proposed for a future css-overscroll level).

experiment.finding: No Origin Trial exists or is planned (ChromeStatus origintrial=false; no OT stage data; no experiment goals/risks fields populated). This is appropriate: the feature is at dev-trial stage behind a flag, and an OT is not justified yet because the uncertainties the team faces (design ergonomics, other-engine interest, accessibility details) are better answered by developer-trial feedback and standards discussion than by a field experiment. 'Not-relevant' here means a field experiment has not been warranted at this stage, NOT that the feature is untested.
experiment.limitations: If wide review surfaces user/population-scale uncertainty (e.g. gesture-vs-browser-navigation conflicts at scale), an OT may become relevant later.

prepareToShip.finding: The feature has not reached prepare-to-ship. ChromeStatus status is 'Proposed', is_released=false; there is no Intent to Ship, no populated rollout/enterprise/support/monitoring plan, and no docs/FAQ/assets. Although the ship stage in the record carries planned desktop_first/android_first of 149, the only actual launch event is the behind-a-flag developer trial at 149. 'Not-relevant' reflects that this phase has not been entered, not that it is unimportant.
prepareToShip.limitations: A '149' milestone appears in both the dev-trial launch event and the (empty) ship stage, which could be misread as shipping; the manifest and ChromeStatus confirm only the dev trial has occurred.

release.finding: The feature has not shipped, and release communication so far is accurate and appropriately hedged. It is correctly absent from the 'New in Chrome 149' shipped-features post (whose highlights are gap decorations, bfcache WebSockets, and Intl.Locale.variants). Its first significant public DevRel mention is the Google I/O 2026 'What's new in web UI' post (2026-07-01), which frames it accurately as 'a proposed declarative solution, in discussion with OpenUI community group' rather than an available feature. This is the right framing for a behind-a-flag trial and avoids the common failure of announcement outrunning implementation.
release.limitations: No reference docs, MDN entry, release-notes item, or demo/article authored for the trial were found; the trial is communicated only via the I/O roundup.

adoption.finding: No adoption evidence exists and none should be expected at this stage: the feature is behind a flag (no default availability), webdx_usecounter_enum is null (no use counter yet), and no framework/library/tool integration or production case study was found. Public mentions are amplification of the I/O 2026 announcement (LinkedIn reshares, a note.com explainer, a Spanish I/O recap), not evaluation or production use. This remains unknown, not negative.
adoption.limitations: Adoption cannot be assessed until the feature reaches at least origin-trial or default availability.

support.finding: No support evidence is available. A tracking bug exists (issues.chromium.org/443719046) but was not retrievable, and no public support issues, known-issues material, or troubleshooting content were found. The feature is too new and too gated to have a support burden signal.
support.limitations: Chromium bug body not retrievable; no support issues or known-issues docs found.

deprecation.finding: This is a brand-new feature with nothing to deprecate or remove; no deprecation/removal path applies. 'Not-relevant' reflects that there is no legacy surface being restricted or replaced, not that removal risk is untested.

developerValue.finding: The developer problem is genuine and recurring (declarative, accessible, script-free gesture UIs such as drawer menus, swipe-to-reveal, and pull-to-refresh), and the design is well-reasoned: it anchors every gesture to a semantic <button> so a keyboard/AT fallback always exists, offloads physics to the compositor, and reuses the proven Invoker Commands mechanism. This is a credible, accessibility-first improvement over JS gesture polyfills. The weakness is evidence: independent developer demand is unevidenced (Web Dev 'No signals'; no framework-author, survey, or partner signal), so value is asserted from problem validity and design quality rather than demonstrated demand.
developerValue.limitations: No attributable developer demand or partner-evidence ladder; 'value' inferred from problem framing + design, not measured.

endUserImpact.finding: Not shipped and no end-user impact study, telemetry, accessibility user-testing, or motion-sensitivity/vestibular evidence was found. The design intends a positive, more-accessible gesture experience, but end-user impact is undetermined.
endUserImpact.limitations: Feature is behind a flag; no end-user data exists.

adoption.finding: No usage counters, framework/library integration, or production deployment evidence exists; too early and too gated to score.
adoption.limitations: Feature not available by default; no adoption denominator possible.

interoperability.finding: Interoperability is the most consequential open risk and is unresolved but being worked. Concrete positives: active, multi-venue standards engagement (Open UI incubation, CSSWG, WHATWG HTML proposal) and reuse of the Invoker Commands pattern that itself is advancing cross-engine. Concrete gaps: Firefox and Safari both 'No signal'; the WHATWG proposal is labeled 'needs implementer interest' and 'stage: 1 Incubation'; the CSSWG spec issue is 'Needs Edits' and 'Needs Testcase (WPT)'; no Baseline path is established. A Chrome-only period is the current default outlook and must not be mistaken for broad success.
interoperability.limitations: No Mozilla/WebKit/TAG positions found; spec not stable; no WPT results confirmed.

implementationQuality.finding: A Chromium implementation exists behind the flag (finch name OverscrollGestures), but its completeness, test coverage, performance, and compatibility could not be verified: ChromeStatus claims WPT:true while the CSSWG issue still requests a WPT testcase, and the tracking bug was not retrievable. No friction-log or conformance evidence was found.
implementationQuality.limitations: Implementation bug and test state not independently verifiable.

evidenceAndCommunication.finding: Communication accuracy is strong: the I/O 2026 post correctly frames the feature as proposed/in-discussion, and it is correctly omitted from the shipped-features Chrome 149 post. The explainer is detailed and honest about open questions. Weaknesses: developer demand is asserted rather than evidenced in the record; the dev-trial instructions and canonical sample were not durably retrievable; and no independent developer evidence dossier accompanies the trial.
evidenceAndCommunication.limitations: Dev-trial instructions and sample not retrievable; no claim/evidence ledger or developer-evidence dossier found.

support.finding: No support burden, issue taxonomy, or known-issues material exists; the feature is too new and too gated to score.

overall.finding: Overall outcome is undetermined and is correctly NOT scored as success or failure. The feature is at the developer-trial stage with sound process ingredients (Open UI incubation, accessibility-first design, accurate and hedged communication, active multi-venue standards work, continuity authors reusing Invoker Commands), but the decisive evidence is missing or pending: no developer demand, zero other-engine interest, all formal reviews pending, spec unstable, and no trial findings. It is premature to call success or failure; the realistic near-term risks are a prolonged Chrome-only period and unvalidated demand.
overall.limitations: Feature pre-shipment; multiple material evidence gaps; outcome will only become scoreable after trial findings, other-engine signals, and review completion.

supports: Feature record, owners, flag/finch, stages, dev-trial at M149, review statuses pending, FF/Safari/WebDev no signal, webdx_usecounter null, is_released=false, status Proposed
sources.limitations: Assessed via the immutable cached snapshot at evidence/chromestatus/features/5261280285949952.json (accurate_as_of 2025-09-08; last updated 2026-04-17). The live chromestatuslite server-rendered view was not separately fetched.

supports: Live summary and motivation text confirming the cached record
sources.limitations: Search-snippet only; full live page not browser-rendered in this run.

supports: Explainer: API (overscrollcontainer + command=toggle-overscroll), goals (no-JS, a11y-by-default, compositor performance), use cases, light-dismiss/modal/a11y/focus behavior, progressive enhancement via popover, alternatives (CSS-only rejected), open questions; created 2026-02-13, last updated 2026-06-04
sources.limitations: Explainer (not a ratified spec); 'last updated 2026-06-04' per page metadata.

sources.limitations: Reader returned the issue body only; comment thread (TPAC discussion) not captured.

sources.limitations: Reader returned the issue body only; subsequent Open UI discussion comments not captured.

supports: WHATWG HTML proposal (2026-03-17, vmpstr) 'Overscroll areas via command=toggle-overscroll'; labels 'addition/proposal', 'needs implementer interest', 'stage: 1 Incubation'; confirms Open UI incubation and a11y-first rationale
sources.limitations: URL normalized to https://github.com/whatwg/html/issues/12273; reader returned issue body only; implementer-interest status is the key unresolved signal.

supports: First major DevRel mention: item #28 'Overscroll gestures (swipeable areas)', accurately framed as 'a proposed declarative solution, in discussion with OpenUI community group' using overscrollcontainer and command invokers; published 2026-07-01

Chrome 149 shipped-features post (2026-06-02) does NOT mention Overscroll Gestures; highlights are gap decorations, bfcache WebSockets, Intl.Locale.variants; consistent with behind-a-flag dev trial, not shipment
sources.limitations: Negative evidence (absence from the post); confirms non-shipment only.

supports: Dev-trial instructions document is referenced by ChromeStatus as the trial's developer onboarding
sources.limitations: Not retrievable (reader returned 500/fetch-failed on blob and raw URLs); content, tasks, and limitations unknown.

supports: Canonical sample/demo is referenced by ChromeStatus
sources.limitations: URL normalized to https://flackr.github.io/web-demos/html/overscrollcontainer; not retrievable (reader timed out); demo behavior/quality not verified.

supports: Tracking/launch bug is referenced by ChromeStatus
sources.limitations: Not retrievable via reader (typically requires authenticated browser); implementation status, tests, and regressions unknown.

sources.publisher: Developer community (note.com, nbellocam.dev, LinkedIn amplifiers of the I/O 2026 session)
supports: Post-I/O awareness amplification referencing the explainer/session; a note.com '2026 Latest' explainer and a Spanish I/O recap (nbellocam.dev) describe the API as in-development
sources.limitations: Amplification of the official announcement, not independent demand, evaluation, or adoption; representative link shown, denominator not enumerable.

successes: Accessibility-first design that anchors every gesture to a semantic <button>, guaranteeing a keyboard/AT fallback and directly addressing a known a11y failure mode of JS gesture polyfills.

Correct venue strategy and genuine iteration: started in CSSWG, moved component semantics to Open UI, and opened WHATWG HTML + css-overscroll proposals; the design deliberately evolved from pure-CSS to HTML attributes + a new toggle-overscroll invoker command reusing the proven Invoker Commands mechanism.

Accurate, well-hedged communication: the Google I/O 2026 post frames it as a proposed solution 'in discussion with OpenUI', and it is correctly omitted from the shipped-features Chrome 149 post, avoiding announcement-outruns-implementation.
Continuity and de-risked pattern reuse: same author team (vmpstr, flackr, mfreed7) that delivered Invoker Commands/popover, lowering API-design risk.
failures: Developer demand is asserted from problem validity, not evidenced: Web Dev 'No signals', and no framework-author, survey, or partner-evidence ladder accompanies the trial.

Interoperability is unresolved and currently Chrome-only in outlook: Firefox and Safari 'No signal'; the WHATWG proposal is labeled 'needs implementer interest' and 'stage: 1 Incubation'; the css-overscroll spec is 'Needs Edits'/'Needs Testcase (WPT)'.
All formal reviews (tag, security, privacy) are pending and no ratified spec exists, so wide-review readiness has not been demonstrated.
Developer-trial outcomes are unevidenced: no cohort, task-completion, friction, or partner findings have been published, so the phase's core questions remain open.
Documentation durability gap: the dev-trial instructions and the canonical sample demo were not retrievable, weakening the trial's onboarding evidence.
counterfactuals: If the team had gathered attributable developer demand (e.g. from framework/library authors building drawers, swipe-lists, and pull-to-refresh, plus app-developer research) before or alongside the prototype, the 'Web Dev: No signals' gap and the WHATWG 'needs implementer interest' label would be easier to close before prepare-to-ship.

If other-engine engagement had been secured earlier (Open UI is the right shared venue, but Mozilla/WebKit have not signaled), the Chrome-only-period and Baseline risk would be materially lower; the current 'needs implementer interest' status shows this has not happened yet.

If the developer trial publishes cohort, task-completion, and friction findings (per phase 03) before wide review, it would answer whether the toggle-overscroll/overscrollcontainer design solves realistic tasks and composes with frameworks, de-risking the next transition.
If the dev-trial instructions and sample were hosted on durable, reader/browser-friendly URLs, trial onboarding and independent verification would not be blocked by dead/unfetchable links.
skillImprovements: Add a phase-02/03 'demand evidence' checkpoint: require at least one attributable framework/library-author or developer-research signal (distinct from problem validity) before recommending wide review, so 'Web Dev: No signals' is an explicit blocker rather than silently accepted.

Add an interop early-warning rule: when ChromeStatus records Firefox/Safari 'No signal' AND a WHATWG/CSSWG issue is labeled 'needs implementer interest', the prepare-to-ship/readiness packet must surface the Chrome-only-period and Baseline risk explicitly and must never treat 'No signal' as neutral/positive.

Add a dev-trial artifact-completeness check: count a developer trial as 'launched' only when the instructions and a live sample are fetchable and linked; flag unreachable trial docs (devtrial.md, demo) as a documentation-durability defect to fix before wider recruitment.

Add a regression-eval rule for pre-shipment features: adoption, end-user-impact, implementation-quality, and support dimensions default to 'unscored', and overall completion is 'partial' when reviews are pending and no other-engine signal exists — codify what this run does so success is never inferred from shipment, usage, press sentiment, or absent criticism.

Add a spec/test consistency check: when ChromeStatus claims WPT:true but a linked standards issue is labeled 'Needs Testcase (WPT)', flag the discrepancy in the wide-review packet rather than reporting test readiness as supported.
